title: Sensing Cursor and Selection
summary: Mouse position, hover target, caret, and highlighted text are high-signal clues because they reveal what the user is attending to right now.
analogy: The cursor is a flashlight beam on the interface: wherever it points, attention and possible intent become brighter.
body:
## First principles
Human attention leaves traces in the interface. We point, hover, click, drag, select, and place the text caret. These traces are cheap, immediate clues about intent.

The problem for an agent is grounding. “Fix this,” “summarize that,” or “click there” are incomplete without knowing what “this,” “that,” and “there” refer to. Cursor and selection sensing convert vague references into candidate targets.

## Plain definition
Cursor sensing reads the pointer location and, when possible, the UI element under it. Selection sensing reads highlighted text, selected files, active objects, or a text caret range.

The flashlight analogy works because a cursor narrows the search space. It does not prove intent, but it says where to look first.

## Signal hierarchy
A useful priority order is:

1. **Explicit selection** — highlighted text or selected object.
2. **Focused control** — active input, active file, active cell.
3. **Recent pointer action** — click, drag, hover, or cursor movement.
4. **Current pointer location** — useful but weaker if stale.
5. **Nearby screen content** — labels and containers around the target.

This prevents overreacting to a cursor that was simply left in the corner.

## Mental model
The diagram shows two streams. Pointer coordinates go through hit testing to find a hover target. Selected text and caret state become intent clues. The agent fuses them before choosing the next action.

## Real examples
Anthropic computer use makes cursor grounding concrete: a model may inspect a screen, move the cursor, click a button, and type. To do that well, it must connect a desired action to a coordinate or UI target.

Windows UI Automation and Apple accessibility APIs improve on raw pixels by exposing control roles, names, bounding boxes, and actions. That means “button under cursor” can become “Submit button with press action,” not just “blue rectangle at x=412.”

## Drills
A user says “translate this” while the cursor is over an image caption but text is selected in the paragraph above. Which signal wins? Usually the selected text, unless the user explicitly mentions the image.

A user says “what is this button?” while hovering over a toolbar icon. What extra context should the agent fetch? The accessible name, tooltip, nearby labels, and app/window title.

## Teach-it-back
Cursor and selection are intent signals because they show where the user’s attention is. Selection is usually stronger than hover because it is deliberate. A robust agent uses hit testing and accessibility metadata to turn coordinates into real UI targets.
diagram:
flowchart LR
  M[Mouse position] --> H[Hit test]
  H --> TARGET[Hover target]
  SEL[Selected text] --> INTENT[Intent clue]
  CARET[Text caret] --> INTENT
  TARGET --> FUSE[Fuse signals]
  INTENT --> FUSE
  FUSE --> NEXT[Next best action]

examples.what: The model can move a cursor and click buttons, so cursor coordinates and target identification become part of grounding an action.
Windows UI Automation can expose bounding rectangles, control types, and patterns, helping map a cursor coordinate to a semantic UI element.
macOS accessibility clients can inspect roles, titles, children, and actions for interface elements instead of relying only on raw mouse coordinates.

drills.q: Why is selected text usually stronger evidence than the full page text?
drills.a: Selection is an active user signal: the user deliberately marked that span as relevant.
Mapping a screen coordinate, such as the cursor position, to the UI element under that point.

drills.q: Name one failure mode of cursor sensing.
drills.a: The cursor may be parked somewhere unrelated, so combine it with recency, focus, selection, and user wording.

teachItBack: Cursor and selection signals are attention signals. The cursor points at possible targets; selection marks the exact text or object the user cares about. Use them as strong hints, but fuse them with focus, recency, and the user’s words before acting.

sources.title: Anthropic — Claude 3.5 models and computer use
Apple — Accessibility Programming Guide for OS X

glossary.definition: Reading pointer position and possibly the UI element underneath it.
Highlighted text, selected objects, or active caret range that indicate the user’s current focus.
A lookup that maps coordinates to a UI element or bounding box.

quiz.q: A user says “explain this” while a paragraph is highlighted. What should the agent privilege?
quiz.explanation: Selection is the clearest local intent signal.

quiz.q: Why should cursor position not be used alone?
choices: It can be stale, accidental, or unrelated to the task
It cannot be represented as coordinates
quiz.explanation: The pointer may be parked; combine it with selection, focus, and user request.
